speaker: interviewer
text: Hilda, you were talking about going to art school. Before we go to that, I'd like to-- to find out, when did you find out what happened to your family? What did you find out, the ones that were left in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after the war was over, we had the news that-- well, we didn't really have any news. We just didn't hear from anybody. We kept asking various agencies to help us to try to find traces of our relatives. And everybody [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was gassed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we subsequently found out that my grandmother and aunt and uncle died in Lublin. Where my other relatives died, some in other camps, I really don't know. How did find this out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Vienna and found out, because I was looking for them. And they had records of my aunt and uncle-- my aunt and uncle and my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the records?

speaker: interviewee
text: The records were in some official office in Vienna. I don't remember exactly where, but in some government office. And they told us it was Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A-- cousins who lived in Czechoslovakia and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My little cousin was Walter, Walter Kohn, and his mother Shari, and a number of other cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel going-- being in Austria? And when did you go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back in 49; 49 the first time. I enjoyed seeing the destruction. All around the building where I lived [BACKROUND NOISE] was rubble. And the opera was in rubble. And I enjoyed that. I felt they deserved it and that was the right thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we went back to the building where I lived and saw some of former neighbors. And they said, do you see what they did to us? [LAUGHS] And I said, what they did to you? I-- They said, isn't it terrible? Look at the destruction and the rubble and everything. I said, well, what do you think you did to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I loved going back and seeing my hometown, and had this love/hate relationship, you know. I-- it's very hard to describe.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you've been there since.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I've been there several times, because my aunt came from Israel. And I still have cousins that went from Israel back to live in Vienna, which I can hardly understand, but they are living there. In fact, one second cousin, a young man, is working for the Austrian government and has a very high position, married to a Gentile girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Elan Fellman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's go back now. You were talking about school. What did you do with your art education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I at first was what they derogatively called a housewife artist. I went to art school and I went to art classes. And I started exhibiting at art fairs and started selling my work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I lived in a small suburb of Chicago called Highland Park, Illinois. And they had a newspaper, And they were desperate for news, so they used to call me and ask me, did you win any prizes? Did you do anything lately? Did you go on any trips, or anything like that? So I would always get write-ups.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the YWCA asked me if I would teach art classes there, and I did. And I taught classes for children and adults. And it got so that my husband said if that writes you up one more time, I'm going to cancel the subscription. [LAUGHS] But it was very good and they really helped make me into a professional artist. I forgot to tell you that when I was in Vienna-- and I was always good at drawing and painting-- at age 10 we had a contest for Halloween to do a picture of Halloween. Now, Halloween in Austria, in a Catholic country, is a Catholic holiday. And it's All Saints and All Souls Day. And everybody goes to the cemetery and processions, with lanterns and beautiful wreaths of flowers. And our maids were always Catholic, and they would take me as a child. From the time I was very little, they would take me to the major cemetery. And there would be all these flower ladies [BACKROUND NOISE] and-- with the beautiful wreaths and sitting there with the umbrellas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I painted a picture of that, the great big wall of the cemetery, with a huge statuary peeking over the wall with crosses of the graves and the wreaths of flowers. And then I said, gee whiz. I'm Jewish. Why do I paint all those crosses? So in addition to the crosses, I put [NON-ENGLISH] on every-- on every grave. And I won first prize in the city of Vienna, because at that time it was a Socialist government and they saw the humor in it. So-- and after that, I thought I had to become an artist. Now, you said you had-- you have one daughter. And what is her name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Diana. And I have one granddaughter, Melissa.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have two wonderful step-grandsons. One is brand new, and his name is Noah Sutton-Smolin. And I have another six-year-old, Max. And we're very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left Chicago in 1975 and moved to Laguna Beach.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of things do you enjoy doing in your spare time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't really have any spare time. [LAUGHS] I paint. And for the last seven years, I've been working for Carnival Cruise Lines doing murals and paintings for the ships. And I have a new commission for a new ship coming out in 1998 or 1997, late '97 or '98. And it will be called And I'm going to do 20 murals, which is 60 large panels. And I do abstracts for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your religious affiliation today?

speaker: interviewee
text: None. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We have tried. We've joined a temple. And we have gone to a meeting of the Jewish Humanistic society. And none of it really has fit my husband and me. We have divergent opinions. We haven't really settled on anything yet. He is more traditional than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- have you belonged to any synagogues or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have, a number of them, especially when my daughter was little. And she hated Sunday school so much we had to change temples.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your husband. When were you married, your current husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married two years ago, and very happily so. And I consider myself very fortunate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his name is Herman Slutzky. And he is just the most wonderful person in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Chicago and lived most of his life in Mattoon, Illinois, a small town outside of Chicago, near the University of Chicago, and came to California I think in 1973 with his first wife, who died several years ago, about the same time when my second husband died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My second husband was named Norman Pierce. And he was a radio and TV announcer in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What What was the reason that you wanted to tell your story today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the major reason really was as a legacy for my family. And we were very recently, just two weeks ago, in China and-- on a cruise. And we met a man there who lectured on his experience as a refugee in Shanghai. And his name is Fred Marcus. And he had given witness and made a tape and asked me if I would be interested. And that's how it came about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived? I mean, what made you keep on going, obviously during Austria and since then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, you may have heard about survivor's guilt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's a big question, why did I survive? I don't know. I really don't know. I was just lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you keep going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what made me keep going? Well, first of all, I was very young. And I had a tremendous sense of wanting to live. And I was just fortunate that in my various endeavors I had these miraculous results, which were really something that nobody could foretell, because I was so fortunate in meeting the American consul and meeting Mr. Jaretzki and meeting the people in England that helped my parents out and saved their lives, and then coming to the United States and having telephone book relatives helping me and meeting these wonderful men that I was married to.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about the way you lived your life and what's important to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very difficult question. I'm not prepared [LAUGHS] to answer that. I should have more time to think about that. What's important to me is to be as a person as I can be and to have mores that are important and try to be an important, good citizen of the United States and help out where I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your experiences in Austria effect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It effects every moment of every day that I live. I can never forget and I can never forgive. I had experiences in Austria when I went back to study with Oskar Kokoschka, who was a very famous artist in Salzburg, Austria, where I met with anti-Semitism and Nazis all over again in 1962. And [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm just thrilled to death that I don't have to live there with these people that I can never forgive and who really have never changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about it often, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time. It's with me all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever dream about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was married the first time in 1941, for 10 years I used to wake up screaming at night. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I dream about it very seldom now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you dream?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, lately all I've been dreaming is about the trials and tribulations of my granddaughter, who has become a hippie. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about when-- in your nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what I dream about, and the problem of the young people in America. And I'm dreaming about the presidential election and fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about when you were dreaming-- when you had your nightmares, what would you dream about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hitler experience of being caught, of confronting Hitler and would I have the nerve to kill him? And that was a constant dilemma in my dreams. What would I do if I met him? And subsequently, I had these dreams in Laguna Beach, when we were living so close to Nixon, what would I do if I met him on the beach? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Have there any-- been any decisions that you made as a result of your experience in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: My whole life is a decision of the experience that I had. I mean, you can't divorce yourself from that. Everything is a decision about that. I don't own a German car. I don't own a German thing. I don't buy anything German. I don't like to speak German, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] although I love the language. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'm not kindly disposed toward anything German.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the way you've raised your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was a very young child when she heard about the terrible things that happened. When the Eichmann trial was on in Israel, she saw these television programs. And she says, that didn't really happen, Mother, did it really? Was that really true? Could that really be true? She tried to be in total denial about it. She didn't want to hear about it. She doesn't want to know anything about it. It hurt her so much when she was little about seeing us crying about my grandmother, my aunts, and my relatives that she was very damaged by that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her religious affiliation?

speaker: interviewee
text: None.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's been over 50 years--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --since your experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: More than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What message would you like to leave for your daughter, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] your grandchild, and anybody that is watching this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: My message is that they should just keep loving each other and remember me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just that I am so extremely lucky that I have this wonderful husband who supports me emotionally tremendously. And I just feel so lucky, both in my profession and my life, in my family. And my husband's children are wonderful and we're very close with them, so I'm really one of the most fortunate people in the world. So-- and I don't feel like I'm 75 years old. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We want to thank you very much. What we're going to be doing now is showing your photographs and bringing your husband on. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hilda, can you introduce me to your husband please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Herman Slutzky, my beloved.

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Is there anything, Hilda, that you would like to say to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what you want me to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything that you would like to share with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think listening to the tape was interesting to you too, because you really never heard anything like that in detail before. So in a way, this was also for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. It was a revisit for me, too, much of the deep detail.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's it been like living with each other, and obviously living with Hilda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's a-- I'm a counterpart of what she said, as far as I'm concerned. I feel the same way about her that she does me. We've been very fortunate to find each other. And she's-- she's the love of my life and we enjoy life every day, make the most of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else that you would like to say to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Speak a little louder. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. We want to thank you very much. And what we're going to be doing here is showing photographs of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful. Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you for--

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather, Adolf Kohn. The picture was taken in 1921. And behind him is the picture of my grandmother, Theresa [? Schlesinger ?] Kohn. And she was a woman that never hurt a fly--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: --who died in concentration camp. They were both born in what is now Czechoslovakia and lived in this small town outside of Vienna, Austria. I don't remember the name of the girl on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do you-- where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the second from the left. This was the occasion of a children's ball in Vienna in 1929. And my cousin Fritz Fellman is the Pierrot next to me. And then my best friend Nora Reisinger, who was in several camps during the war, is next to my cousin Fritz. The two children on the end, I think one was Lisa Reif on the left. And the boy on the right, I don't remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your name, first and last please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name, Hilda Pierce.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time it was Hilda Harmel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1929 in Vienna. My mother on the left, her name is Eveline Harmel, nee Kohn. I'm in the middle, the fat one, and my father Julius to my right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your first and last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hilda [INAUDIBLE] Harmel at the time. And this was taken in 1930, when I was nine years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: City and country.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna, Austria. And they put me on a stool so that we're all the same height.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in the schoolyard, which is more like a prison yard, in Vienna. And it was called [NON-ENGLISH] in the Fifth District. I'm the third one on the right, third in from the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your first and last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hilda [INAUDIBLE] Harmel. And my homeroom professor, Dr. Schubert, and next to him in the dark suit, the school principal, Dr. Mashek, both of whom became very ardent Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1935. This is my first and last Austrian passport. It was dated 1934 and it expired in 1939. And after that, I became stateless. And the name there is Hilda Harmel. My aunt and uncle, Camilla Grunwald and [INAUDIBLE] Grunwald, [? Jakob ?] Grunwald. They both perished in concentration camps. And this is a picture taken in 1938, before I left Vienna. This is the picture that saved my life. I sent this picture to America asking for affidavits. It was taken in 1938 after Hitler annexed Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first and last name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, Hilda [INAUDIBLE] Harmel. [INAUDIBLE] This is my wedding picture in 1941. Hilda Harmel, then Rubin, and my first husband Charles Rubin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Chicago. And we were married in Hannibal, Missouri. We eloped. I was widowed in 1969 and met Norm Pierce, who is on my left. And this is a picture just before we were married in 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Norm Pierce and Hilda Pierce. This is my daughter, Diana, Diana Ray Rubin Daly, my only child, taken in 1975.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York. She was working there. This is my foster mother in England on her 100th birthday, and she's still alive. She's 103 and sharp as a tack.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1993 when I visited her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Marjorie Rosevere. My wedding picture, January 11th, 1994. Rabbi Raklis on the left, then my husband Herman, my son-in-law Rocky.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rocky Smolin. And Max Benjamin and his wife Marsha, my lovely stepdaughter; the bride and-- the ancient bride; my daughter Diana Daly; my granddaughter Melissa Daly; and my son-in-law Everitt Daly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you're talking about the bride, that is you and you're-- and you're wearing the green. And your first and last name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hilda Pierce.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what city was-- country?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Irvine, California. I-- I had this picture taken after I finished my 17 murals for the ship called the One of them is behind me. I'm in my garage studio.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name, please first and last?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hilda Pierce. And

speaker: interviewer
text: The city and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken in Laguna Niguel, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1995. Herman and me, on board the off the coast of Japan. And that was taken two weeks ago in 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is-- your first and last name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hilda and Herman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still use my name Pierce, my professional name, and Herman Slutzky, my husband.
